sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=22, col=34).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 119, level 2.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'SPACE': 'Changes board state (timer/HUD) but does NOT complete level (VERIFIED steps 32-113)', 'MOUSE': 'Click dot to select (white border), click another dot to swap colors (VERIFIED step 40); cross-rect dot clicks swap colors between red and green rects (VERIFIED step 75)', 'ACTION7': 'Unknown action - gives error when called (VERIFIED step 104)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'top_panel': '7 colored squares at row 1: [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M] - likely target pattern or palette', 'red_rect': 'Red-bordered container at rows 18-25 with 3 paintable dots at cols 21,27,39', 'green_rect': 'Green-bordered container at rows 30-41 with 4 paintable dots at cols 21,27,33,39', 'T_shape': 'Light green (N) connector between red and green rects', 'bottom_squares': '7 gray squares at row 58 - NOT interactive (VERIFIED step 103)'}
- HUD map: {'timer_bar': 'row 53, charcoal bar that changes with SPACE', 'bottom_squares': 'rows 58, gray squares, not interactive'}
- Win pattern: {'unknown': 'SPACE does not complete level even with correct-looking arrangements. All traversal orders tried and failed.'}
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Paint 4 dots with bottom colors, SPACE to submit', 'level_2': 'In progress - 113 actions, 6+ arrangements tried, SPACE never completes'}
- Cross-level notes: {'level_2_mechanic': 'Swapping colors between dots via click-select then click-swap. Cross-rect swapping works.'}
Current level:
- World model: Current: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]. Tried Green L→R + Red R→L traversal matching top row [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]. SPACE still fails.
- Goal model: Find correct arrangement or discover mechanic beyond arranging dots. All simple traversal orders exhausted.
- Recent findings: ['ACTION7 gives error', 'Bottom gray squares not interactive', '6 different dot arrangements + SPACE all failed', 'Only SPACE and MOUSE are functional actions']
- Open questions: ["Is there a correct arrangement I haven't tried?", 'Does the goal involve something other than matching the top row?', "Could the T-shape or borders be interactive in a way I haven't tried?", 'Could the white corner pieces be clickable?']
- Plan: Try clicking on white corner pieces of red rect, or try completely different non-arrangement approach
- Strategy log: ['Tried Red L→R + Green L→R: failed', 'Tried Green L→R + Red L→R: failed', 'Tried column-major reading: failed', 'Tried reversed orders: failed', 'Tried T-shape path traversal (Green L→R, Red R→L): failed', 'All 6 arrangement hypotheses exhausted - need fundamentally different approach']
- Failed probes: ['SPACE at step 32 (before all dots painted)', 'ACTION7 (error: unknown action)', 'MOUSE(22,34) click c dot (no effect)', 'MOUSE(8,40) click top N (no effect)', 'SPACE x3 at steps 35-37 (no color shift, no submit)', 'MOUSE(8,10) click top O while dot selected (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,p,R], Green=[b,N,Y,M] (step 53)', 'SPACE after Green=[O,p,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'SPACE after Green=[p,O,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'MOUSE(38,21) then MOUSE(38,27) palette clicks + SPACE', 'MOUSE(58,10) bottom square click (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,R,Y], Green=[p,b,N,M] (column-major)', 'SPACE after Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b] (T-shape path)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 103 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The T-dot click also had no effect. Let me look at the segmentation more carefully:
Key objects:
- id=0 color=c pixels=2404 - background (charcoal), children include white corners and other objects
- id=1 color=B pixels=148 - top panel background (black), children are the 7 top row colored squares
- id=18 color=R pixels=70 - red rect border (red color!)
- id=19 color=c pixels=140 - this is charcoal, contains red rect dots (children=[20,21,23])
- id=22 color=N pixels=98 - T-shape connector (light green), children=[24,27] where 24 is the charcoal T-dot and 27 is the green rect border
- id=27 color=c pixels=144 - green rect border (charcoal), children=[28,29,30,31] (the 4 green rect dots)
- id=33 color=G pixels=53 - timer bar (dark gray)
- id=34 color=c pixels=612 - bottom area (charcoal), children are the 7 gray squares
Wait, I see something interesting:
- id=18 color=R pixels=70 - this is RED, and it's the red rect border!
- The red rect border is RED (R color)
- The green rect border is id=27 color=c (charcoal)
But wait, looking at the image, the red rect's border looks reddish and the green rect's border looks greenish. Let me reconsider...
Actually, id=19 color=c pixels=140 contains the red rect dots [20,21,23]. And id=18 color=R pixels=70 is separate. So maybe:
- id=18 (R, 70 pixels) is the RED part of the red rect border
- id=19 (c, 140 pixels) is the charcoal part that contains the dots
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me look at the containment hierarchy:
- id=0 (c, background) contains [16,17,18,19,22,25,26]
  - 16,17,25,26 are white corners
  - 18 is red border (R)
  - 19 is charcoal container with dots [20,21,23]
  - 22 is N (T-shape) containing [24,27]
    - 24 is charcoal T-dot
    - 27 is charcoal green rect border containing [28,29,30,31]
Wait, so the green rect border (id=27, color=c) is a CHILD of the T-shape (id=22, color=N)? That's strange. And the T-shape is a child of the background.
Also, id=19 (c, 140 pixels) contains the red rect dots [20,21,23], and id=18 (R, 70 pixels) is separate. So the red rect has:
- A red border piece (id=18, R, 70 pixels)
- A charcoal container (id=19, c, 140 pixels) with dots inside
And the green rect has:
- A charcoal border (id=27, c, 144 pixels) with dots inside
- But it's contained within the T-shape (id=22, N, 98 pixels)
This is getting complex. Let me focus on what I can interact with.
I've tried:
1. All arrangements + SPACE - failed
2. White corners - no effect
3. Top row palette - no effect
4. T-dot - no effect
5. SPACE with dot selected - no effect
6. Bottom squares - no effect
What haven't I tried?
- Clicking on the rect borders themselves (not corners, not dots)
- Clicking on the T-shape (not the dot, but the N-colored part)
- Multiple SPACE presses in sequence
- Clicking on the background
Wait, I just noticed something. The red rect border has a RED component (id=18, color=R). What if clicking on the red border does something?
Also, let me reconsider the problem. What if the goal is not about arranging dots at all, but about:
1. Matching the border colors to the dot colors
2. Or moving dots between rects
3. Or something else entirely
Let me try clicking on the red rect border (the R-colored part). From the segmentation, id=18 is color=R with 70 pixels at center (23,31). The border is around the red rect, so let me try clicking on the top edge of the red rect border.
Actually, let me think about this differently. Looking at the image:
- The red rect has a RED border (the top edge looks red)
- The green rect has a GREEN border (looks light green)
- The T-shape is also light green
What if the goal is to have the dots INSIDE each rect match the border color?
- Red rect: all 3 dots should be red (R)
- Green rect: all 4 dots should be light green (N)
But we only have one R and one N among the 7 dots. So that can't work with swapping alone.
Unless... the mechanic allows DUPLICATING colors? What if clicking on a border while a dot is selected copies the border color to the dot?
Let me try: select a dot, then click on the red rect border to paint it red.
Actually, I just realized I should look at this from a completely different angle. What if the goal is about the NUMBER of dots in each rect?
- Red rect has 3 dots
- Green rect has 4 dots
- Total: 7 dots = 7 colors in top row
What if the goal is to have exactly the right number of dots in each rect, and the dots should be sorted by some property?
Or what if the red rect should have "warm" colors (O, R, Y) and the green rect should have "cool" colors (p, b, N, M)?
- Warm: O, R, Y (3 colors) → red rect (3 dots)
- Cool: p, b, N, M (4 colors) → green rect (4 dots)
This would make sense thematically! Red = warm, Green = cool.
Current state: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
- M is cool (magenta)
- Y is warm (yellow)
- N is cool (light green)
- O is warm (orange)
- p is cool (purple)
- R is warm (red)
- b is cool (blue)
So warm colors: O, R, Y (should be in red rect)
Cool colors: p, b, N, M (should be in green rect)
Current warm in red: Y only (need O, R too)
Current cool in red: M, N (should be in green)
Current warm in green: O, R (should be in red)
Current cool in green: p, b (correct)
So I need to swap:
- M (red) ↔ O (green) → M goes to green, O goes to red
- N (red) ↔ R (green) → N goes to green, R goes to red
After swaps: Red=[O,Y,R], Green=[M,p,N,b]
Warm in red: O, Y, R ✓
Cool in green: M, p, N, b ✓
Let me try this arrangement!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# T-dot click also had no effect
# Let me try a completely different hypothesis: warm/cool color sorting
# Warm colors (O, R, Y) → red rect (3 slots)
# Cool colors (p, b, N, M) → green rect (4 slots)
# Current: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
# Need: Red=[O,Y,R] or [O,R,Y], Green=[M,p,N,b] or similar

# Check current dot positions and colors
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
dots = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 16 and n['color'] not in ['c', 'g', 'G', 'W', 'B']:
        b = n['boundary']
        cr = sum(r for r,c in b) // len(b)
        cc = sum(c for r,c in b) // len(b)
        dots[(cr, cc)] = n['color']
print("Current dots:", dots)

# Need to swap M(22,22)↔O(36,22) and N(22,40)↔R(36,34)
# Swap M with O: click M, click O
# Swap N with R: click N, click R
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},  # select M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},  # swap with O
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40},  # select N
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},  # swap with R
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Only changes timer, never completes level", "MOUSE": "Click dot to select, click another to swap colors", "ACTION7": "Error"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "7 colored squares at row 3", "red_rect": "3 dots", "green_rect": "4 dots"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row 53"}, "win_pattern": {"unknown": "Need new hypothesis"}, "level_log": {"level_2": "119 actions"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]", "goal_model": "Trying warm/cool color sort: warm(O,R,Y)→red, cool(p,b,N,M)→green", "recent_findings": ["T-dot click no effect, border colors: red rect has R border piece"], "open_questions": ["Is warm/cool sort the goal?"], "current_plan": "Swap to get warm colors in red rect, cool in green", "strategy_log": ["All arrangements failed, trying color property sort"], "failed_probes": ["All arrangements + SPACE", "ACTION7", "bottom squares", "white corners", "top row palette", "SPACE with dot selected", "T-dot click"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Unless... the mechanic allows DUPLICATING colors? What if clicking on a border while a dot is selected copies the border color to the dot?
Let me try: select a dot, then click on the red rect border to paint it red.
Actually, I just realized I should look at this from a completely different angle. What if the goal is about the NUMBER of dots in each rect?
- Red rect has 3 dots
- Green rect has 4 dots
- Total: 7 dots = 7 colors in top row
What if the goal is to have exactly the right number of dots in each rect, and the dots should be sorted by some property?
Or what if the red rect should have "warm" colors (O, R, Y) and the green rect should have "cool" colors (p, b, N, M)?
- Warm: O, R, Y (3 colors) → red rect (3 dots)
- Cool: p, b, N, M (4 colors) → green rect (4 dots)
This would make sense thematically! Red = warm, Green = cool.
Current state: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
- M is cool (magenta)
- Y is warm (yellow)
- N is cool (light green)
- O is warm (orange)
- p is cool (purple)
- R is warm (red)
- b is cool (blue)
So warm colors: O, R, Y (should be in red rect)
Cool colors: p, b, N, M (should be in green rect)
Current warm in red: Y only (need O, R too)
Current cool in red: M, N (should be in green)
Current warm in green: O, R (should be in red)
Current cool in green: p, b (correct)
So I need to swap:
- M (red) ↔ O (green) → M goes to green, O goes to red
- N (red) ↔ R (green) → N goes to green, R goes to red
After swaps: Red=[O,Y,R], Green=[M,p,N,b]
Warm in red: O, Y, R ✓
Cool in green: M, p, N, b ✓
Let me try this arrangement!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# T-dot click also had no effect
# Let me try a completely different hypothesis: warm/cool color sorting
# Warm colors (O, R, Y) → red rect (3 slots)
# Cool colors (p, b, N, M) → green rect (4 slots)
# Current: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
# Need: Red=[O,Y,R] or [O,R,Y], Green=[M,p,N,b] or similar

# Check current dot positions and colors
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
dots = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 16 and n['color'] not in ['c', 'g', 'G', 'W', 'B']:
        b = n['boundary']
        cr = sum(r for r,c in b) // len(b)
        cc = sum(c for r,c in b) // len(b)
        dots[(cr, cc)] = n['color']
print("Current dots:", dots)

# Need to swap M(22,22)↔O(36,22) and N(22,40)↔R(36,34)
# Swap M with O: click M, click O
# Swap N with R: click N, click R
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},  # select M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},  # swap with O
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40},  # select N
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},  # swap with R
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Only changes timer, never completes level", "MOUSE": "Click dot to select, click another to swap colors", "ACTION7": "Error"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "7 colored squares at row 3", "red_rect": "3 dots", "green_rect": "4 dots"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row 53"}, "win_pattern": {"unknown": "Need new hypothesis"}, "level_log": {"level_2": "119 actions"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]", "goal_model": "Trying warm/cool color sort: warm(O,R,Y)→red, cool(p,b,N,M)→green", "recent_findings": ["T-dot click no effect, border colors: red rect has R border piece"], "open_questions": ["Is warm/cool sort the goal?"], "current_plan": "Swap to get warm colors in red rect, cool in green", "strategy_log": ["All arrangements failed, trying color property sort"], "failed_probes": ["All arrangements + SPACE", "ACTION7", "bottom squares", "white corners", "top row palette", "SPACE with dot selected", "T-dot click"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
